stem: Let \( X \), \( Y \), and \( Z \) be independent random variables with standard deviations \( [[sx]] \), \( [[sy]] \), and \( [[sz]] \), respectively. What is \( \mathrm{Var}(X + Y + Z) \)?
solution: For independent variables the variances add, and each variance is the square of the standard deviation: \( \mathrm{Var}(X+Y+Z) = [[sx]]^2 + [[sy]]^2 + [[sz]]^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the standard deviations first and then squares; standard deviations of independent variables do not add that way.
This adds the standard deviations instead of the variances.
This subtracts one variance; for independent variables all variances add, even for a difference of variables.
This is off by one from the correct variance.

stem: Let \( X \) and \( Y \) be independent random variables with standard deviations \( [[sx]] \) and \( [[sy]] \), respectively. What is \( \mathrm{Var}(X - Y) \)?
solution: For independent variables, variance is always ADDITIVE — even for a difference: \( \mathrm{Var}(X-Y) = [[sx]]^2 + [[sy]]^2 = [[answer]] \); the minus sign inside \( X-Y \) does not flip the sign between the two variances.
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the standard deviations first and then squares, mimicking how the values themselves are subtracted.
This subtracts the variances, assuming a difference of variables makes variance subtract as well; variance is never negative-additive here.
This subtracts the standard deviations directly without squaring anything.
This expands \( (X-Y)^2 \)-style but keeps a spurious cross term instead of the true sum of variances.
This is off by one from the correct variance.

stem: Let \( X \) and \( Y \) be independent random variables with standard deviations \( [[sx]] \) and \( [[sy]] \), respectively. What is \( \mathrm{Var}([[a]]X + ([[b]])Y) \)?
solution: Scaling a variable by a constant scales its variance by the constant squared, and independent variances still add: \( \mathrm{Var}([[a]]X+([[b]])Y) = [[a]]^2\cdot[[sx]]^2 + [[b]]^2\cdot[[sy]]^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the coefficients \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \) before scaling each variance.
This squares the entire combination \( aX+bY \) as if it were a single already-added standard deviation, introducing a spurious cross term.
This subtracts the second scaled variance instead of adding it, even though the coefficient \( [[b]] \) is squared elsewhere.
This computes the corresponding linear combination of standard deviations rather than of variances.
This is off by one from the correct variance.

stem: Let \( X \) and \( Y \) be independent random variables with \( \mathrm{Var}(X) = [[vx]] \) and \( \mathrm{Var}(Y) = [[vy]] \). What is the standard deviation of \( X + Y \)?
solution: The variances of independent variables add first, and only THEN do you take the square root to get the standard deviation: \( \sigma_{X+Y} = \sqrt{[[vx]] + [[vy]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two individual standard deviations directly; standard deviations of independent variables do not add that way.
This is the variance of the sum, without taking the final square root.
This reports only \( X \)'s standard deviation, ignoring \( Y \) entirely.
This reports only \( Y \)'s standard deviation, ignoring \( X \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct standard deviation.

stem: Let \( X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{[[n]]} \) be independent random variables, each with standard deviation \( [[sigma]] \). What is \( \mathrm{Var}(X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_{[[n]]}) \)?
solution: Each of the \( [[n]] \) independent variables contributes its own variance \( [[sigma]]^2 \), and independent variances add: \( \mathrm{Var}\left(\sum X_i\right) = [[n]]\cdot[[sigma]]^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the sum as if it were \( n \) copies of the SAME variable added together (\( (n\sigma)^2 \)), rather than \( n \) independent variables.
This forgets to square the standard deviation before multiplying by \( n \).
This reports only a single variable's variance, forgetting the other \( n-1 \) variables entirely.
This confuses this sum with a sample-mean standard error, scaling by \( \sqrt{n} \) instead of \( n \).
This is off by one from the correct variance.
